19 Plaintiff DR. PHILIP BALLA alleges as follow:
20 ||. y
21 GENERAL ALLEGATIONS :
‘ 1. Plaintiff (hereinafter “Dr. Balla”) is a natural person residing in the
. 22 State of California, in the County of San Francisco.
23 >. On information and belief, defendant GOLDEN GATE UNIVERSITY
24 is anon profit business entity, with its headquarters located in the State of California,
25 in the County of San Francisco.
%6 3. On information and belief, BARBARA KARLIN is a natural person doing
99 ; business in the State of California, in the County of San Francisco, and is employed by ;
defendant GOLDEN GATE UNIVERSITY in the capacity of Vice President of
28 Academic Affairs.

 
an ra C
1 .
3 4. On information and belief, TERRI SHULTIS is a natural person doing
business in the State of California, in the County of San Francisco, and is employed by
. 4 defendant GOLDEN GATE UNIVERSITY as Director of Human Resources.
- 5 5. On information and belief, LES GOTTESMAN is a natural person doing
G business in the State of California, in the County of San Francisco, and is employed by
7 defendant GOLDEN GATE UNIVERSITY in the capacity of Humanities and English ‘
8 Department Chairperson.
9 6. Defendants DOES ONE through TWENTY, inclusive, are individuals who .
10 are responsible for the acts and omissions alleged below. Dr. Balla is presently
unaware of the identities of DOES ONE through TWENTY, inclusive, and therefore
i sues them with fictitious names.
12 7. Allacts and omissions alleged herein substantively occurred in the State of
~ 13 California, in the County of San Francisco. Venue and jurisdiction are therefore |
14 proper in this forum.
15 8. Dr. Balla began working for defendant Golden Gate University in and
16 around September, 2001, as an English tutor at the graduate and undergraduate level.
. ‘ Dr. Balla served in this capacity regularly, with excellence and without incident, well
174i into his seventh, or what university tradition construes as sabbatical year.
18 9. Dr. Balla has a Ph. D. (University of Michigan, 1977), and has worked
19 professionally, and with distinction, for over 30 years, in at least five countries, in
20 ||. human rights, and in the fields of writing, editing, and teaching. -
21 10, While representing the United States as first Fulbright lecturer at the .
0 farthest-eastern university in Czechoslovakia — before, during, and after the 1989 fall
33 of communism there — Dr. Balla saw how higher education in eastern Europe did not
| challenge, but reinforced the dccper hatreds and misunderstandings of latent
24 ; nationalisms: 1) stressing specializations in university departments otherwise isolated
25 |}. from each other; 2) promoting the veneers of impersonality; and 3) enforcing the
26 |. platitudes of Marxism-Leninism as cultural commonality. Dr. Balla proceeded to
97 obtain foundation monies and began what was central and eastern Europe’s first-ever .
28 program to rotate guest intellectuals from the neighboring region before student

 
f c C | |
1
3 audiences to explore personal and cultural connections: “Other Cultures — Inner
Values,” at the main university in Budapest, Hungary, 1991-92.
_ 4 | 11. From these experiences, Dr. Balla saw how literacy may better engage the
5 personal and the cultural, his evolving program hereafter called Essaying Differences.
6 The “Proprictor’s Column” at www, FesavineDifferences.com shows his seeing how
7 American universities, too, reduce literacy, in reducing bridges between the personal
g and the cultural. The reigning conceits of the former communist states pale before our |
9 | own orthodoxies for 1) departments mutually-isolating by specializations; 2) .
instructors and students alike equating “professionalism” with impersonality conceits;
10 ‘ and 3) administrators fitting the same entitlement culture inside universities as
i Americans practice outside as entitlement consumerism.
12 12. For these years’-long experiences, and as shown in his “Proprietor’s
13 Column” essays, Dr. Balla alleges FACT ONE: that he was well-prepared to teach
14 the fall 2007 course at defendant Golden Gate University, and well-knew the :
15 challenges students would feel for that nexus of literacy, personal involvement, and
16 cultural reference.
. ' 13. Following his unblemished track record going into his seventh, or
17 i sabbatical year, tutoring with defendant Golden Gate University, Dr. Balla was hired
18 by defendant Golden Gate University in mid-summer 2007 to teach Graduate Writing,
19 English 301, for the fall 2007 semester. Approached for this, he immediately stressed
20 it. to the Department Chair, defendant Les Gottesman, that his course would have os
1 students acknowledging, quoting, and referring to each other —a use of personal and
2 cultural linkages otherwise unknown in the academe which students have long seen as
relentlessly impersonal in all its sequestered departments.
23 14. Prior to the commencement of the fal! 2007 English 301 course, Dr. Balla
24 submitted first draft of his Course Syllabus to the Department Chair, defendant Les
25 Gottesman. As tutor at defendant Golden Gate University, Dr. Balla had worked with
26 | dozens of students taking this English graduate writing course from various instructors
“97 for going on seven years. He well knew its parameters. Still, for his own proposed
28 syllabus, he carefully reviewed sample syllabi from this course’s previous instructors.
a

 
a: c CO |
1 .
4 15. Dr, Balla also sent a copy of his first draft syllabus to the immediately i
previous instructor, Carol Jameson (no longer affiliated with Golden Gate University,
a but experienced in having taught English 301 for many years).
5 16. Dr. Balla then accommodated the suggestions for revisions — none
6 tendered from Dr. Jameson, these from Chair Gottesman all for student happiness. Dr.
7 Balla then submitted his final Course Syllabus by e-mail to the appropriate offices at ;
g defendant Golden Gate University, and delivered a paper copy to the office of
9 defendant Gottesman. As FACT ONE remained true — that Dr. Balla was eminently
10 prepared to modify the course for literacy’s challenges linking the personal and the
cultural — and as his proposed course well honored the standards of those before it,
ae Course Syllabus was accepted without further revision, well before the course began. |
12 17. After teaching the class for approximately one month, Dr. Balla was
7 13 relieved from his teaching duties October 5, 2007, by defendant Gottesman for
14 ambiguous reason. In his e-mail of October 5, 2007, Chair Gottesman cited only .
15 “circumstances” and tacitly admitted that he was “still gathering information” in
16 relation to the substance of the allegations against Dr. Balla.
; 18. Dr. Balla thereafter made several verbal and written requests to be
aE informed of the exact allegations and to address the charges in a cordial environment
OBE with the defendant Golden Gate University’s staff.
19 19. On November 28, 2007 Dr. Balla was informed via a letter from defendant
20 I; Terri Schultis, Director of Human Resources, that Plaintiff had been accused of: os
21 a. Allegation 1): Dr. Balla made derogatory remarks about one or more of
. n the students’ ethnic background, thereby creating a hostile environment.
. “ b. Allegation 2): Dr. Balla made specific remarks of a sexual nature,
29 regarding masturbation.
24 c. Allegation 3): Dr. Balla invited students to his home in such a way as to
25 |. give the appearance that Dr. Balla’s motives included establishing a
26 |. romantic relationship with the students.
- 99 20. Asto Allegation 1), while the November 28, 2007 letter claimed
28 “sufficient evidence” that it was “likely” Plaintiff had made derogatory and
unacceptable remarks to students; specificity as to the evidence was withheld. As to
re

 
w
we ¢ C ,
1 ;
2
3 the charge of discrimination in Allegation 1), the November 28, 2007 letter cited
insufficient evidence to support a claim of discrimination.
_ 4 21. The only statements Plaintiff made in reference to Allegation 1) regarding
5 “derogatory” and “ynacceptable comments” were items read by the students from the
6 Course Syllabus’s three authors, George Onvell, W. H. Auden, and A. J. Liebling, and
7 from the students’ writings and oral comments on their own cultural backgrounds. ‘
8 22. As to Allegation 2), Dr. Balla on information and belief asserts that his
9 English 301 students confused the word “emasculating” from the first paragraph of . |
; one of the three assigned readings with the very-different word “masturbation.”
10 93. Dr. Balla further asserts, as to Allegation 3), that he offered further
i assistance outside the classroom — either in the Writing Center of defendant Golden
12 Gate University, where he regularly provided in-person tutoring, going on seven years,
. 13 or at his home, for one, or two students at a time, as any liked. Dr. Balla lives at his
14 home with a long-term partner, Sue Noda, and over the years often had students over Oe
15 if any needed extra help. This offer stemmed from Plaintiff's love of and dedication
6 to teaching, and had no sexual innuendo ever.
. ‘ 24. The defendants at Golden Gate University never at any time showed
7 i interest in or were willing to discuss the ways the Course Syllabus challenged students
18 into the legitimate vulnerability whereby good teachers often key advancement. On
19 information and belief, defendants instead only made inquiries, requested, solicited,
20 |. and made statements, both verbal and in writing, to and from faculty and students ae
21 alike, advancing baseless hysteria. Their attention to emotionalism only, completely
7 - severed from all pedagogic context, trumped proper educational mission for slander.
” These defendants cared not for the educational challenges the students had with stirred
23 emotions already embraced, but aimed instead only at creating a written record
24 inflating the aforementioned unsubstantiated allegations leveled against Dr. Balla.
25 25, Requesting a meeting with defendant Barbara Karlin, Dr. Balla on
26 |. December 5, 2007, received a letter from her admitting the baseless nature of the
97 charges that defendant Golden Gate University had indulged, she writing, “To recap,
28 the finding of the inquiry indicated that the alleged behaviors did not constitute sexual
harassment or discrimination.”
a

 
o c C |
53 26. Defendant Karlin, like the others, however, chose to indulge her own i
4 personal bent for perceived miscreant emotions, and for these totally out of the
, approved educational context that stirred them. In her December 5 letter she stressed
5 her and the other defendants’ fear that the students had a “stated intention of all
6 ; dropping the course” — oblivious to the fact that, for dropping any course, students at
7 defendant Golden Gate University never had to present any complaints: neither for
8 the legitimate skill deficiencies students may have, nor for the illegitimate slanders
9 rogue administrators may stir up for their own illegitimate bents to hysteria. . |
10 27. The defendants’ repeatedly stressing their fear that the students had a
“stated intention of all dropping the course” should invite attention to the fact that the
uM students were indeed stirred and challenged, even as, for these challenges, they were
12 unprepared emotionally and in skills. The students went to administrators not to drop
"13 the course, but in evidence of FACT TWO: that they were engaged in the challenges
14 newly stirred in them exactly as they were just beginning to feel their lack of resources
15 for them. Dr. Balla, as first party in this action, had begun what he had planned — and ,
- 16 as FACT ONE shows him to have been qualified to set in motion. The students, as
. 17 second party in this action, deserved to learn the literacy for these challenges, as the |
Course Syllabus set. The administrators, as third party in this action, should have
18 joined students’ right to learn to instructor’s approved role to teach.
19 28. As part of defendants’ concern for student emotions, defendants expressed
20 Il. outrage that Dr. Balla had never taken the sexual harassment training course that
21 . defendant Golden Gate University had available. Dr, Balla had never taken this
ay) course — it turned out to be an online exercise, of less than two hours’ duration —
23 because he had never heard of it. Inhis going-on seven years of steady employment at
Golden Gate University, neither of his supervisors ever indicated to him the existence
24 . of sucha course. Defendant Golden Gate University never advertised this coursc, nor
25 |. the need for employees to take it. Defendant Golden Gate University never posted
26 |. "notices on the walls about it, as it often posted notices of other issues. It never wrote
27 any memo or letter to Dr. Balla or, on his information and belief, to any of his tutoring
28 peers. When Dr. Balla learned of the sexual harassment training, and that it was
available (only) online, he took it immediately, and scored a 100% perfect. This
a

 
<°* °
2
3 This perfect score demonstrated Dr. Balla’s knowledge and absolute professionalism
in this area — which defendants totally ignored, for the emotionalism of their own
/ 4 : slander campaign, their emotions and slanders beyond any interest in facts.
5 29, As part of defendant Golden Gate University’s indulging emotions apart
6 from facts, and apart from educational context, at no stage was Dr. Balla provided
7 access to any of the aforementioned unsubstantiated allegations. He was not formally.
8 informed of the aforementioned unsubstantiated damaging allegations in writing until
9 after a decision by the defendants had been rendered. : |
10 30. The aforementioned unsubstantiated damaging allegations and statements
by defendants, written and oral, are materially false and have caused substantial
ae damage to Dr. Balla’s professional standing in the community, and have brought
12 tangible economic damages.
~ 13 31. Asa result of the aforementioned written and oral false statements by
14 defendants, Dr. Balla has Jost at least $38,000 in income and suffered irreparable
15 damages to his unblemished reputation as an educational professional. ,
- 16 32. Asaresult of being subjected to the aforementioned written and oral false
‘ statements, Dr. Balla has suffered a diminished professional reputation, a diminished
aa community reputation, and other general damages in an amount to be proven at trial. |

ey | CAUSE OF ACTION
20 |. SLANDER a
21 33. Dr. Balla incorporates by reference and realleges all above paragraphs. ,
‘ 34, On information and belief, during the course of the investigation into Dr.
- 22h. Balla’s alleged conduct, defendants deliberately made and induced false oral
23 unprivileged statements conceming the aforementioned allegations against Dr. Balla
24 when they accused him of “sexual harassment/discrimination [as a result of] a
25 response to an informal complaint.”
%6 . 35. During the investigation stemming from “an informal complaint,” .
“99 defendants’ oral questions and responses were made, and the investigation was
conducted in bad faith. The defendants ignored the actual pedagogical context which
28 stirred legitimate student emotions. The defendants ignored their own failure to

4 advertise their sexual harassment training course. They then ignored this course's :
results which, when Dr. Balla leamed of the course, and promptly took it, only showed
_ 4 his perfect knowledge and professionalism in this area. Dr. Balla’s supervisors in his )
5 two tutoring jobs at Golden Gate University, Janet Goldberg and Gwen Ball, both
6 knew of his totally excellent and professional work — the former his supervisor at the
7 drop-in Writing Center for going on seven years. As third parties — neither in any way ;
. 8 connected to Dr. Balla’s graduate writing course — both supervisors were approached |
9 by the defendants, so both could tell that only suspiciously unprofessional criteria ;
; underlay defendants’ tilt to hysteria.
10 36. Alternately, during the investigation stemming from “an informal
, i complaint,” defendants’ oral questions and responses were made, and the investigation
12 was conducted negligently and in such a manner that a reasonable person and/or
" 13 business entity would have known the likelihood of causing the damages that in fact
14 occurred.
15 37. On information and belief, defendants made the aforementioned false ;
- 16 statements and accusations with a reckless disregard for the truth, and knew, or should
. ‘ have known, the falsity of these statements.
17 4 38. On information and belief, defendants knew, or should have known, the i
7 18! constant excellence and repute of Dr. Balla’s work, for going on seven years — well
19 into his sabbatical year. Defendants knew, or should have known, that the tradition of
20 I. university sabbatical year vouches for tenure in the professional work one has been .
91 doing — tenure the mark of respect for long-proven excellence. Defendants had a duty .
2 to respect Dr. Balla’s long-proven excellence, a duty they breached when, to his
“ supervisors — third parties in no way connected to the graduate writing class — the
23 defendants made the aforementioned false statements and 2ecusations regarding Dr.
24 Balla. Defendants went to these outside parties, extending their slanders as the only
25 reason Dr. Balla be given no further work. Though both supervisors recognized the
6 patent illegitimacy of what they were told by the defendants, the defendants directed
99 the supervisors to comply, and never to reveal their third-party discussions.
28 39, Defendants had a preexisting duty to Dr. Balla to handle this most serious
matter in a professional manner, to include defendant Golden Gate University’s sexual
eS

 
‘¢ .
2
3 | harassment policy, but not to ignore 1) their own failure to advertise their sexual 4
4 harassment training course, 2) Dr. Balla’s perfect score in that course, When belatedly
alerted to his need to take it, and 3) the context of legitimate challenges Dr. Balla was
5 honoring in the Course Syllabus that these same defendants originally approved.
6 40. Defendants’ oral statements to members of the faculty, and their
7 discussions with students, tended directly to injure Dr. Balla in respect to his office, ;
3 position, trade, and business, by imputing a reference that directly attached to his
9 profession, trade, and business in such a way as has a natural tendency to lesson his .
10 profits and standing in the professional community.
41. Additionally, because defendants Barbara Karlin, Terri Schultis, Les
i Gottesman, and Does One through Twenty were presuming to act within the scope of
12 their employment and to be enforcing defendant Golden Gate University’s sexual
" 13 harassment policy, defendant Golden Gate University is vicariously liable for the
14 slanders of defendants Barbara Karlin, Terri Schultis, Les Gottesman, and Does One
15 through Twenty under respondent superior principles.
- 16 A2. Alternatively, because defendants Barbara Karlin, Terri Schultis, Les
. W . Gottesman, and Does One through Twenty, were, for their slanders, acting outside the |
‘ scope of their employment with defendant Golden Gate University in their handling of
18 defendant Golden Gate University’s sexual harassment policy, defendants Barbara
19 Karlin, Terri Schultis, Les Gottesman, and Does One through Twenty are liable to Dr.
20 I}. Balla as well as Golden Gate University under respondent superior principles. .
21 ,
PUNITIVE DAMGES
22) 43. Dr. Balla incorporates by reference and realleges all above paragraphs.
23 44, By engaging in the aforementioned conduct, defendants, and each of them,
24 acted maliciously and despicably, subjecting olaintiff to a cruel and unjust hardship.
25 45. Plaintiff is therefore entitled to punitive damages from defendants, and
261 each of them, in an amount to be proven at trial.

. 1. for compensatory damages in an amount to be proven at trial;
